coredata.dc:title: A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yu Zhu
Zhang, Ying
Li, Na
Liu, Shi Gang
Liu, Ting
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel water-soluble and blue fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized using one-pot hydrothermal method with malonic acid and urea, and were then used as a label-free fluorescent probe for selective, sensitive, and rapid determination of picric acid (PA). The sizes of the as-prepared C-dots were mainly distributed in the range of 1.5–3.0nm with an average diameter of 2.5nm. Moreover, the C-dots displayed blue fluorescence with an emission peak at 395nm (320nm excitation), which also showed good stability and anti-photobleaching ability. Interestingly, the fluorescence of C-dots could be selectively quenched by PA within 10s. Based on this phenomenon, a label-free fluorescence assay was proposed for specific determination of PA. The fluorescence intensity of probe showed a linear response to PA in the concentration range of 0.1 to 26.5μM with a low detection limit of 51nM (S/N=3). Finally, the resultant fluorescent probe was successfully applied to the detection of PA in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Picric acid
Fluorescence quenching
Inner filter effect
Electron transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of PA with C-dots
Selectivity study of C-dots-based fluorescent probe for PA detection
Detection of PA in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration about synthesis process of fluorescent C-dots and detection principle for PA. Insets: photographs of the aqueous solution of C-dots in the absence (a, b) and presence (c, d) of PA under visible light (a, c) and 365nm UV light (b, d). C (C-dots) =20mg/mL; C (PA) =5.5μM.
(A) Typical TEM image of C-dots. The inset displays the HRTEM image. (B) Histogram of the size distribution of C-dots obtained from TEM image analysis. C (C-dots) =10mg/mL.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of C-dots. Insets show the photographs of C-dots under visible light (a) and UV light (b). (B) FTIR spectra of C-dots. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400nm in 10nm increments. C (C-dots) =100mg/mL.

(A) Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0-F)/F 0 of C-dots by PA in different pH buffers. (B) Normalized fluorescence intensity of C-dots with PA at different incubation time. C (C-dots) =24mg/mL; C (PA) =5.5μM; λex =320nm, λem =395nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots with different concentrations of PA (from top to bottom, 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.9, 1.3, 5.3, 6.6, 8.8, 10.6, 13.3, 26.5, 55.4, 69.3, 92.3, 138.5, 227.0μM). Inset: the relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and the PA concentration. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots with different concentrations of PA within the linear range of 0.1-26.5μM. Inset: the linear plot of (F 0-F)/F 0 and the concentration of PA. Conditions: BR buffer (pH 7.0); C (C-dots) =20mg/mL; λex =320nm.

Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0-F)/F 0 of C-dots in the presence of different nitroaromatics and aromatics. F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the absence and presence of nitroaromatics and aromatics, respectively. Conditions: pH 7.0, BR buffer; C (C-dots) =20mg/mL; C (PA) =11μM; C (interferences) =100μM; λex =320nm, λem =395nm.
Determination of PA in water samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel water-soluble and blue fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized using one-pot hydrothermal method with malonic acid and urea, and were then used as a label-free fluorescent probe for selective, sensitive, and rapid determination of picric acid (PA). The sizes of the as-prepared C-dots were mainly distributed in the range of 1.5–3.0nm with an average diameter of 2.5nm. Moreover, the C-dots displayed blue fluorescence with an emission peak at 395nm (320nm excitation), which also showed good stability and anti-photobleaching ability. Interestingly, the fluorescence of C-dots could be selectively quenched by PA within 10s. Based on this phenomenon, a label-free fluorescence assay was proposed for specific determination of PA. The fluorescence intensity of probe showed a linear response to PA in the concentration range of 0.1 to 26.5μM with a low detection limit of 51nM (S/N=3). Finally, the resultant fluorescent probe was successfully applied to the detection of PA in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatic explosives, as a kind of typical hazardous material, can cause environmental pollution, health hazards, and potential homeland security threats [1]. Thus, rapid and sensitive detection of nitroaromatic explosives is an important research topic. In the past decades, several highly effective experiments have already been performed for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) [2–7]. However, the other nitroaromatic explosives rarely got attention such as picric acid (PA). As an important member of nitroaromatic explosives, PA has attracted considerable research interests only in recent years [7–12]. It has strong acidity for electrophilic effect of nitro and possesses more violent explosive power than TNT. Due to good water solubility, it can lead to serious environmental contamination and endanger human health through soil and groundwater. Therefore, accurate determination of nitroaromatic explosive PA is of great significance.

In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots), as a novel member of nanomaterial family, have attracted tremendous attention due to their favorable performances. The C-dots possess various merits including outstanding optical properties, robust chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, and eco-friendliness [13], which promote their versatile applications in many fields. Among them, C-dots-based sensors were successfully applied in chemical analysis. Functionalized C-dots have been used to follow pH values [14], or to sense molecules such as glucose [15], dopamine [16], and ascorbic acid [17]. It was also demonstrated that metal ions, including Cu2+ [18], Hg2+ [19], Fe3+ [20], Cr6+ [17,21], and Au3+ [22], could quench the fluorescence of C-dots. As for the detection of PA with C-dots, there were also some literature reported. However, two aspects of the detection performances need to be improved. For one thing, some reported C-dots exhibited non-selectivity towards PA [23]; for another, the sensitivity was not so good that it should be further improved [24,25]. Therefore, exploring simple and effective sensors based on fluorescent C-dots for sensitive and selective determination of PA is highly desired.

Herein, we first synthesized blue fluorescent C-dots by heating urea-malonic acid mixture. The as-prepared C-dots can serve as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of PA in aqueous solution.

All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade. Urea, malonic acid, H3PO4, HAc, H3BO3, NaCl, PA, phenol (PHE), aniline (AN), and methylbenzene (MB) were supplied by Chongqing Chuandong Chemical (group) Co., Ltd., China. NaOH, 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), p-nitrotoluene (p-NT), m-dinitrobenzene (m-DNB), o-nitrophenol (o-NP), nitrobenzene (NB), o-dihydroxybenzene (o-DHB), p-dihydroxybenzene (p-DHB), Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (Bu4NPF6), and benzoic acid (BA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Ultrapure water (18MΩcm) was used in all experiments.

Urea (0.6008g) and malonic acid (1.0403g) were dissolved with ultrapure water (10.00mL), and the mixture was then treated hydrothermally in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200°C for 4h. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, the colorless and transparent solution was subjected to dialysis (molecular weight cut off=3.0kDa) for 1day. Finally, the resulting material was dried by lyophilization to obtain yellow C-dots viscous fluid for further characterization and use.

The morphology and microstructure of C-dots were analyzed using a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100 JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained using a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Ultraviolet visible (UV-vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were carried out using a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The pH values of solutions were measured with a PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai Leici Instrument Company, Ltd., China). All electrochemical measurements were monitored by a CHI660 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CHI Instruments Co., China). The electrochemical system is a conventional three-electrode cell, which contains a glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode, a platinum electrode as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (sat.KCl) reference electrode.

The quantum yield (QY) of C-dots was measured by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of C-dots samples with quinine sulfate. In order to minimize re-absorption effect, the absorbance of C-dots samples was kept below 0.10. Quinine sulfate with QY=0.54 was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index of 1.33) while C-dots were dissolved in ultrapure water (refractive index of 1.33). The QY of C-dots was measured by the following equation:
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
+
(
I
S
/
I R
)
×
(
A
R
/
A S
)
×
(
η
S
2
/
η R
2
)
where Φ is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance measured on the UV–vis spectrophotometer and η is the refractive index. The subscript S and R refer to sample and reference, respectively.

At room temperature, 50.0μL of C-dots was mixed with 450.0μL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH 7.0) to keep the system under a constant pH condition. Subsequently, 5.0μL of PA standard solution with different concentrations was added in the system, and the mixture was gently shaked for 10s. The mixture was then moved into a quartz cuvette, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 320nm. The reagent blank experiment was carried out under the same conditions.

Carbamide contains nitrogen and malonic acid is rich in carbon and carboxyl groups. Both of them are cheap and ecofriendly materials, so it is a cost-effective way to synthesize C-dots by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization process with urea and malonic acid. The preparation process of C-dots is shown in Scheme 1 . The possible formation mechanism of C-dots is as follows: The dehydration-condensation reaction occurred firstly in the precursors solution during the heating process. Afterwards, the carbonization process happened, and finally the C-dots with some functional groups formed. This process is similar to that of some other reported C-dots [26,27].

The size and morphology of the as-prepared C-dots were characterized by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the C-dots are spherical and well separated from each other. The sizes of C-dots are mainly distributed in the range of 1.5-3.0nm with a mean diameter of 2.5nm as judged from over 100 individual particles (Fig. 1B). The inset of Fig. 1A shows the well-resolved lattice fringes with an average interplanar spacing of 0.24nm, which is very close to the (100) lattice spacing of graphite (d 100 =0.24nm) [26,28]. The good monodispersed property and the high crystallinity of C-dots could be attributed to the constant, simultaneous, and homogeneous heating of solvent (H2O), urea, and malonic acid, which provided uniform nucleus growth for the formation of C-dots [28,29].

The optical properties of C-dots were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. S1, compared to unheated mixed solution without any obvious UV–vis absorption in the wavelength range of 300–500nm (Fig. S1A, curve a) or fluorescence emission (Fig. S1B, curve a), the resultant C-dots show a typical absorption peak at 340nm (Fig. S1A, curve b), and a bright fluorescence emission of C-dots is observed at 395nm (Fig. S1B, curve b). In brief, the well-defined UV–vis absorption peak of C-dots at about 340nm was from n-π* transitions and resulted in strong emission due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states [13,30,31]. Furthermore, the maximum emission of C-dots centered at 395nm (Fig. 2 A, curve Ex) with 320nm excitation (Fig. 2A, curve Em). As shown in the inset b of Fig. 2A, the C-dots emit bright blue fluorescence under a 365nm UV light. The above results indicated that highly fluorescent C-dots were successfully synthesized.

The functional groups of C-dots were then detected by FTIR specrosopy. As shown in Fig. 2B, the dull and intense band of 3417cm−1 was assigned to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The band at 1664cm−1 represented CO stretching vibration [32], and 1408cm−1 was assigned to CN stretching vibration [33]. The υ (CO) and υ (CN) peaks indicated the formation of amide bonds on C-dots surfaces [34]. The band at 1125cm−1 should be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO [35]. The above observations confirmed that the as-synthesized C-dots were functionalized by hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH), and amino groups (–NH2), which could not only improve the stability and hydrophilicity of C-dots in an aqueous system, but also play an important role in picric acid detection with C-dots as a functionalized fluorescent probe.

The fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots under various excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2C. When the excitation wavelength varies from 290 to 400nm, the maximum emission wavelength shifts from 370 to 450nm. However, the fluorescence intensity decreases as the emission peak red-shifts. The C-dots exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties. The origin of the phenomenon could be related to the surface states. As evidenced from the FTIR spectrum, the as-synthesized C-dots have different kinds of functional groups like CH, CO, CNH2, and COH on the surface which could form a series of energy levels in surface states. These energy levels might result in corresponding emissive traps [36]. When C-dots were excited by an illuminant at a certain wavelength, a kind of surface state emissive trap would dominate the emission. Consequently, different excitation wavelengths could give rise to different emission behavior, resulting in the excitation-dependent properties [29,36]. Excitation-dependent properties were commonly found in fluorescent carbon nano-materials [37], which provided multicolor fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths and were potentially important for certain practical applications.
The QY of C-dots in aqueous solution was calculated to be 12.6% at 320nm excitation, using quinine sulfate (QY=54%, in 0.1M H2SO4) as the reference.

The as-obtained C-dots showed good stability. As shown in Fig. S2, little decrease of fluorescence intensity is observed under continuous excitation (365nm) for 70min with a UV lamp, indicating that the C-dots displayed the property of good anti-photobleaching. In addition, the C-dots were stable for over 3 months when stored at 4°C. As described in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity and the characteristic peak of C-dots don’t change obviously in different concentrations of NaCl solutions. The stability of C-dots guarantees their applications under sophisticated and harsh conditions.

Earlier research pointed out that electronic conjugate structures [31], free zig-zag sites [38], functional groups [39], quantum-confinement effect [40], or surface defects [41] might be the origin of PL emission. In our case, the absorption near 250nm corresponding to the π-π* transition produced nearly no PL signal, suggesting that the PL of C-dots should not be produced by the electronic conjugate structures [31]. As described from FTIR spectrum, the C-dots have different kinds of functional groups on the surfaces. The surface states were induced by the hybridization structure of the surface groups, and then the formed different surface defects could introduce different emission sites onto C-dots [42]. When the C-dots irradiated, surface defects trapped excitons and then released energy in the form of photons emission. Thus, the PL of C-dots was attributed to the radiative recombination of trapped electrons and surface defects on C-dots surfaces [37].

In order to obtain optimal fluorescence properties of C-dots, many synthesis reaction conditions including molar ratio of reactants, reaction temperature, and reaction time were optimized. As illuminated in Fig. S4, the molar ratio of urea to malonic acid greatly affects the fluorescence intensity of C-dots, and the optimal concentration ratio of urea to malonic acid is found to be 1:1. Urea and malonic acid polymerized through the dehydration between carboxyl and amino groups, and thus the smaller the difference of the concentration between urea and malonic acid is, the more carboxyl and amino groups participated in the polymerization and then carbonized to form more C-dots, which resulted in stronger fluorescence emission [43].

As shown in Fig. S5, low temperature leads to weak fluorescence intensity because of the incomplete reaction of urea with malonic acid. With increasing temperature, the reaction is more complete, resulting in the enhanced yield of C-dots [30]. In addition, the bright fluorescent C-dots can be obtained with increasing reaction time. It is possible that long reaction time promotes the polymerization and carbonization of reactants, leading to the increase of the fluorescence intensity of solution. Considering not only optimal fluorescence property of C-dots but also cost and energy conservation, the eventual reaction temperature and time were designated as 200°C and 4h, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of PA with C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanism for the fluorescence detection of PA with C-dots, some optical properties of C-dots and PA were investigated. As displayed in Fig. S6, the wide overlaps between the absorption spectrum of PA and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of C-dots suggest that the fluorescence decrease may arise from the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [24,44]. As illustrated in Fig. S7, only very small change of fluorescence decay time from 3.95 to 3.90ns occurred before and after the addition of PA. The fluorescence lifetime has almost no change, which was in accordance with literature report [45–47]. Different from FRET, two species in the IFE process should not chemically interact with each other and the quencher does not change the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore [24,48]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism could be IFE rather than FRET. Although IFE is usually considered as a source of error in fluorometry, it has been applied as a developing novel fluorescent assay in recent studies [49,50]. There is no denying that IFE is an important, inevitable, and dominant factor in spectrofluorometry. In the approach, the IFE-based sensors did not require establishing any chemical linking between the fluorophore and the receptor but utilize the fluorophore and the receptor as such, which offers considerable flexibility and more simplicity [45,49,51]. As shown in Fig. S8, no new absorption peak could be observed after adding PA, which indicated that no non-fluorescent ground-state complex was formed [52,53]. As the redox ability of the excited-state molecules is stronger than that of the ground-state molecules, the excited-state molecules of fluorescent species are easier to form exciplex with quencher than ground-state molecules, through the process called electron transfer. Different from the formation of non-fluorescent ground-state complex, the formation of exciplex will not change the absorption spectrum of fluorescent species [52,53]. To get further insight into the PL quenching mechanism, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed to investigate the electrochemical properties of C-dots. As shown in Fig. S9, The E red was determined to be −0.56V (Fig. S9A, E red is the onset of reduction potential for C-dots), and the E g was estimated to be 3.29eV (Fig. S9B, E g is the energy band gap resulting from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum of C-dots). The energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of C-dots were calculated as −7.13 and −3.84eV, respectively, according to the empirical formula [54,55]. Meanwhile, the E HOMO and E LUMO of PA could be calculated as −8.70 and −5.82eV, respectively, by the B3LYP method in Dmol3 mode [47]. As shown in Fig. S10, the electron transfer in the excited state of C-dots can occur from the LUMO of C-dots to the LUMO of PA, indicating that electron transfer between C-dots and PA is possible. Therefore, the C-dots exhibited a high fluorescence quenching response toward PA because of both IFE and electron transfer, which is in accordance with the reported literature [47,56].

In order to obtain better detection sensitivity for PA, the optimization of assay conditions including solution pH value and interaction time was necessary. Effect of pH on the sensing performance of C-dots to PA was studied by monitoring the dependence of the fluorescence quenching efficiency, (F 0–F)/F 0, on the pH of BR buffer, where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the absence and presence of PA, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the quenching efficiency maintains within a narrow fluctuation range from 0.71 to 0.74 within the pH range of 2–10. Therefore, the change of pH values had little influence on the sensing performance, and the detection process could be conducted in a broad pH range. Finally, pH 7.0 was selected in the following experiment for a best detection result.

The optimal incubation time for PA detection was investigated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence of PA. It is clear that a stable fluorescence intensity is obtained after 10s incubation (Fig. 3B). Thus a short reaction time of just 10s was selected for the PA determination, which realized the rapid detection of PA.

To investigate the dependence of fluorescence intensity of C-dots upon the concentration of PA, different concentrations of PA were tested with the proposed method under optimum conditions. Fig. 4 A displays that the fluorescence intensity of C-dots at 395nm gradually decreased with the addition of PA from 0 to 277μM. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was proportional to the PA concentration in the range of 0.1−26.5μM. The linear correlation can be described by the linear regression equation (F 0–F)/F 0 =0.017C+ 0.0479 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9956, where C denotes the concentration of PA. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 51nM at a signal to noise ratio of 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study of C-dots-based fluorescent probe for PA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity was examined by monitoring the change of fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence of PA, various nitroaromatics, and aromatics (2,4-DNP, TNT, 2,4-DNT, p-NT, m-DNB, o-NP, NB, o-DHB, p-DHB, PHE, MB, AN, and BA), whose detailed information including molecular weight and structure is summarized in Table S1. As presented in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence of C-dots isn’t evidently quenched by above species except for PA, which demonstrates the good specificity of the fluorescent probe towards PA. The selectivity toward PA could be explained by the IFE mechanism. As shown in Fig. S11, there is little overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectra of the other thirteen nitroaromatics and the fluorescence excitation as well as emission spectra of C-dots, which lead to good selectivity to PA. Considering sensitivity, selectivity, and rapidity of the C-dots-based fluorescent probe for PA determination, our method exhibited superiority compared to previously reported methods (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PA in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the feasibility of the proposed method, the synthesized C-dots were applied to detect PA in real water samples. Water samples were from the tap water of Southwest University. Because no PA was detected in the water samples using the fluorescent probe, a recovery test was done on the samples spiked with PA at different concentrations. The results are shown in Table 1 . It can be seen that the recoveries of PA are in the range of 96.48-116.06%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of three replicate detections for each sample are below 3%. Therefore, the as-prepared C-dots showed an application potential for PA detection in real samples.

In conclusion, we first synthesized and characterized the new blue-emitting C-dots by one-pot hydrothermal method with urea and malonic acid. The obtained C-dots had a narrow size range of 2–3nm, a fluorescence QY of 12.6%, good water solubility, and excellent pH-independent ability. Based on the IFE process and electron transfer between C-dots and PA, a simple method was constructed for the highly sensitive, selective, and rapid detection of PA in aqueous solution using the prepared C-dots as a fluorescent probe without further chemical modification. The assay system showed a linear dependence between the fluorescence intensity of C-dots and PA concentration in the range of 0.1 to 26.5μM. And a low limit of detection of 51nM was obtained. The proposed strategy was facile, simple, and low cost, which exhibited great potentials in biological and environmental sensor applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (CSTC-2013jjB00002, CSTC-2015jcyjB50001), and the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Sichuan Institutes of High Education (No. LYJ1402, LYJ1501).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent detection of TNT and 4-nitrophenol by BSA Au nanoclusters
Self-assembled TNT biosensor based on modular multifunctional surface-tethered components
A hybrid quantum dot-antibody fragment fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based TNT sensor
A surface functional monomer-directing strategy for highly dense imprinting of TNT at surface of silica nanoparticles
A simple assay for direct colorimetric visualization of trinitrotoluene at picomolar levels using gold nanoparticles
Instant visual detection of trinitrotoluene particulates on various surfaces by ratiometric fluorescence of dual-emission quantum dots hybrid
Biodegradation of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by rhodococcus sp. isolated from a picric acid-contaminated soil
Oligotriphenylene nanofiber sensors for detection of nitro-based explosives
Highly sensitive fluorescent imidazolium-based sensors for nanomolar detection of explosive picric acid in aqueous medium
Supersensitive and selective detection of picric acid explosive by fluorescent Ag nanoclusters
Highly selective detection of nitro explosives by a luminescent metal-organic framework
Sensitive detection of picric acid based on creatinine-capped solid film assembled by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and chitosan
Carbon-based dots Co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Carbon nanoparticle for highly sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of mercury(II) ion in aqueous solution
Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on water soluble carbon nanodots with multiple sensing capacities
Porous carbon quantum dots: one step green synthesis vial-cysteine and applications in metal ion detection
Carbon dot cluster as an efficient off-on fluorescent probe to detect Au(III) and glutathione
Induction coil heater prepared highly fluorescent carbon dots as invisible ink and explosive sensor
A facile synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous solution
Amine-capped carbon dots as a nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of picric acid in aqueous solution via electrostatic interaction
A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on L-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Facile synthesis of biocompatible N, S-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and ion detecting
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Investigating the surface state of graphene quantum dots
Blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide
One-step synthesis of hollow carbon nanospheres in non-coordinating solvent
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
One-step synthesis of fluorescent hydroxyls-coated carbon dots with hydrothermal reaction and its application to optical sensing of metal ions
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
To lose is to gain: effective synthesis of water-soluble graphene fluoroxide quantum dots by sacrificing certain fluorine atoms from exfoliated fluorinated graphene
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots through the charge transfer effect of functional groups
Large, solution-processable graphene quantum dots as light absorbers for photovoltaics
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots
A label-free fluorescence sensing approach for selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in aqueous solution using graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
Rapid fluorescence assay for Sudan dyes using polyethyleneimine-coated copper nanoclusters
Green synthesis of fluorescent hydrophobic carbon quantum dots and their use for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection
Highly selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed terbium-doped blue carbon dots
Highly sensitive photoluminescence energy transfer detection for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol using photoluminescent carbon nanodots
Copper ion-selective fluorescent sensor based on the inner filter effect using a spiropyran derivative
A chemosensing ensemble for the detection of cysteine based on the inner filter effect using a Rhodamine B spirolactam
Design of fluorescent assays for cyanide and hydrogen peroxide based on the inner filter effect of metal nanoparticles
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Green light-emitting polyepinephrine-based fluorescent organic dots and its application in intracellular metal ions sensing
PVA-based nanobiosensor for ultrasensitive detection of folic acid by fluorescence quenching

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yu Zhu Fan is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Ying Zhang is a doctoral student candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Na Li is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Shi Gang Liu is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Ting Liu is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.

Nian Bing Li is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and PhD degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Korea. His research interests are the developments of electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.

Hong Qun Luo is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a visiting scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensor.
